planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, fertile soil; Crystal Apple is a New Zealand heirloom cucumber that ripens to a creamy-white to ivory color and round to oval apple shape rather than the typical green cylinder, with thin, non-bitter skin, crisp texture, and mild, sweet flavor that makes it one of the most delicious and heat-tolerant cucumbers for warm-climate gardens.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost in warm soil; Crystal Apple can sprawl on the ground or be trellised — vertical growing keeps fruits cleaner and makes them easier to find. Crystal Apple is a New Zealand heirloom cucumber producing distinctively rounded to oval fruits about the size and shape of a small apple, ripening from green to creamy-white to ivory-yellow; the fruits are tender-skinned with no bitterness at any stage, with exceptionally mild, refreshing, sweet flavor even when large. They are best harvested at the white-to-pale-ivory stage before turning yellow, when the interior is crisp and seeds are still small. An outstanding cucumber for warm-climate gardens: more heat-tolerant than most slicing types, with less bitterness in hot conditions; highly productive and self-trellising if grown on a support. Popular at farmers markets for the unusual appearance and superior flavor.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Dragon Egg is a Croatian heirloom cucumber with egg-shaped, creamy-white to pale yellow fruits harvested before they turn orange, with a mild, sweet flavor, thin skin, and exceptional heat tolerance — one of the most productive and beautiful novelty cucumbers for warm summer gardens.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost in warm, fertile, well-drained soil; train vines on a trellis or fence to make harvesting easier and keep fruits off the ground. Dragon Egg is a Croatian heirloom cucumber with a charming and unusual appearance — the fruits are oval to distinctly egg-shaped, smooth-skinned, and creamy-white to pale yellow when ripe, with a delicate internal structure of mild, sweet flesh and small seeds. They are harvested when fully white-to-cream colored (before they turn orange, which indicates over-maturity and seedy, bitter flesh); at the right stage the flavor is mild, refreshing, and virtually non-bitter even in hot conditions. The plants are extremely productive in warm weather and the fruits hang in clusters, making them easy to spot and harvest. Outstanding for hot summer gardens where standard cucumbers become bitter; one of the most reliably productive white-fruited novelty cucumbers.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Straight Eight is the All-America Selections-winning classic American slicing cucumber of the mid-twentieth century, the standard by which all slicers were measured from its 1935 AAS Gold Medal through the 1980s — still an excellent garden variety with reliable production and the classic cucumber form.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in hills or rows after last frost in warm, fertile, well-drained soil; train vines up a trellis for cleaner fruits or let sprawl in ample space. Straight Eight won the All-America Selections Gold Medal in 1935 and for decades became the definitive American garden cucumber — named for its uniform, straight, exactly 8-inch, dark green fruits with rounded tips, blunt blossom ends, thin skin, crisp flesh, and reliable productivity. It remained the standard garden slicing cucumber through the mid-twentieth century and was the one cucumber every American seed company carried. While modern hybrids have surpassed it in disease resistance and uniformity, Straight Eight remains a beloved heirloom for gardeners who want a reliable, seed-saveable, classic slicer. Produces well in warm conditions; good for Gulf Coast spring and fall production.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Poinsett 76 is a Clemson University breeding program selection from the original Poinsett variety, released in 1976 and still widely grown in the Southeast for its outstanding combination of disease resistance, heat tolerance, and production in hot, humid conditions that challenges most cucumber varieties.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost when soil is above 65°F; provide support or allow vines to sprawl with 3–4 feet between hills. Poinsett 76 was developed by the Clemson University vegetable breeding program in South Carolina and released in 1976 as an improved selection of the original Poinsett cucumber (released 1966) — both named for Joel R. Poinsett, the South Carolinian who brought the poinsettia plant from Mexico. Poinsett 76 is distinguished by its outstanding disease resistance package (angular leaf spot, anthracnose, downy mildew, mosaic, and powdery mildew resistance) and heat tolerance that makes it the cucumber of choice for hot, humid Southeastern gardens where most varieties decline quickly in summer; fruits are 7–8 inches long, dark green, cylindrical, and of good eating quality. An open-pollinated, seed-saveable classic that remains one of the best choices for Gulf Coast and Deep South summer cucumber production.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Vert de Massy is the classic French cornichon pickling cucumber used to produce the tiny, intensely flavored gherkins traditional in French charcuterie and cuisine, harvested at just 1–2 inches long for the smallest and finest quality, or at 2–3 inches for a slightly larger cornichon.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost in warm, fertile, well-drained soil; harvest every 2–3 days to keep plants productive, picking at 1–3 inches long. Vert de Massy (Green of Massy) is the traditional French cornichon gherkin, a small, bumpy-skinned pickling cucumber prized in France for the production of cornichons — the tiny, intensely flavored pickles that accompany pâté, charcuterie, and raclette throughout French cuisine. The fruits are small, cylindrical to slightly tapered, dark green with small warts and spines, and have firm, crisp flesh with excellent flavor even when very small (1–2 inches). Harvest every 2–3 days to keep plants productive — if fruits are allowed to mature they divert energy from new production. Pickled in white wine vinegar with tarragon, chervil, and pearl onions in the traditional French method; also excellent as fresh gherkins. The vines are compact and productive.
